notes.text: The first verse of the Bible gives us a sure and easy truth: that the world was made by a Being of infinite wisdom and power, who was himself before all time. The work of creation is God's work; and he is the first cause of all things.
Moses does not speak of the infinite essence of God, but shows him to us as he is the Creator of heaven and earth. We are to learn that the world was not made by chance, but that God was its Maker.

Man was made last of all the creatures: this was both an honour and a favour to him. Man was made in God's image, after his likeness: pure and upright, resembling God in knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness.
If God brings his people into straits, he will find a way to bring them out. Stand still—think not to save yourselves; leave it to God to work for you. The Lord shall fight for you.

From what David had been to his flocks as a good shepherd, he was led to consider what God is to his people. Care and protection of the sheep belong to the shepherd. With the Lord as our Shepherd, we may be sure we shall not want any thing that is really good for us.
In the darkest and most trying hour God is near. His rod (defense) and staff (support) comfort the saint. Death itself is only a 'shadow,' not the substance of evil to those who are in Christ.

They that wait on the Lord, renewing their strength by faith and prayer, shall be able to do that which is above the measure of their natural ability. God can renew the strength of those who, by waiting on him, fetch in supplies of grace.

Our sins were the thorns in Christ's head, the nails in his hands and feet, the spear in his side. He was wounded for our transgressions—he was bruised for our iniquities. By his stripes we are healed.
The good which God requires of us is not expensive sacrifices, but to do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with our God. These are more acceptable than thousands of rams.

What is meant by 'the poor in spirit'? The humble and contrite in mind. For by 'spirit' he means the soul and the faculty of choice. Blessed are they who are not high-minded, who are not puffed up with pride.
The poor in spirit are happy. They are humble and lowly in their own eyes. They see their want, bewail their guilt, and thirst after a Redeemer. The kingdom of grace is composed of such.

The call is free: Come. The character of those invited: all that labour and are heavy laden. The blessing promised: I will give you rest. Rest for the soul is the most desirable rest—to be eased of a burden of sin.

While all the other Evangelists begin with the Incarnation, John, soaring higher, speaks of the eternal existence of the Word. 'In the beginning was the Word'—he was not made, for nothing that was made was before him.
The eternal and intimate relation of the Son of God to the Father is here declared. The Word was with God, and the Word was God. From all eternity the Son had glory with the Father.

Here is gospel indeed—good news. God so loved the world—not as he loves his own children, yet with a common love of pity and patience. He gave his only-begotten Son that whosoever believes should not perish.

Christ brought life because the heavenly Father loves the human race and wishes that they should not perish. Faith is not a bare knowledge, but receives Christ as he is offered by the Father—for salvation.
Christ is the way: we cannot come to God as our Father but by him as Mediator. He is the truth: as spoken of by all the prophets. He is the life: our spiritual and eternal life is in and from him.

That is good for the saints which does their souls good. Afflictions and death itself are not good in themselves, yet God works them together for good to those that love him and are called according to his purpose.
All that happens to God's people is so overruled as to promote their spiritual and eternal good. This is not said of all men, but of those that love God—the called according to his purpose.

Do not be conformed to this world. Conversion and sanctification are the renewing of the mind—a change not of the substance, but of the qualities of the soul. Prove what is that good and acceptable will of God.

The more excellent way is charity—true Christian love. It is patient and kind; not envious, proud, or selfish. It thinks no evil, rejoices in truth, and endures. Without this, the greatest gifts are nothing.
Every converted sinner is a saved sinner: delivered from sin and wrath. Grace is the original source; faith the means—and even that not of ourselves; it is the gift of God. Boasting is excluded.

Be careful for nothing—unduly anxious. Pray about everything. Thanksgiving must be joined with prayer. The peace of God shall keep your hearts: a garrison of divine peace about the mind in Christ Jesus.
Joy in God is always our duty. Continual prayer is the way to keep that joy. In everything give thanks—for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you.
Faith is the firm belief that God will perform all he has promised in Christ. It is the substance of things hoped for—it makes them real to the soul—and the evidence of things not seen.
Love is of God. He that loves not, knows not God—for God is love. The Spirit of God is the Spirit of love. Those who live in hatred and malice declare that they know not God.

The presence of God with his people in heaven will not be interrupted as it is on earth. No more death, sorrow, crying, or pain—the former things are passed away. He that sits on the throne makes all things new.

All power is given to Christ; therefore he is able to protect and reward his servants. Go and disciple the nations—baptizing and teaching them. And lo, I am with you always: his presence is the strength of the church.
